nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shorelines, coral reefs, boulder fields, and tide-pool zones from the subtidal fringe down to about 15 meters, typically sheltering inside rock crevices where the flattened carapace wedges tightly into small spaces. In its native range the Sally Lightfoot Crab is a dedicated algae grazer, feeding almost exclusively on algae growing on rock surfaces. It is also an established invasive in the Mediterranean, where it has spread rapidly since first being recorded there in 1999 and is widely regarded as one of the most invasive decapod crustaceans to enter that sea.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical/subtropical rocky reef and rubble zone

decorPreferences: abundant live rock with crevices and ledges to wedge the flattened carapace into
rubble and boulder-style hardscape mimicking a rocky shoreline
open sand or fine substrate for foraging
good water flow — this is an active, current-loving species
tightly fitted lid recommended — climbs rockwork agilely and can leave open tanks

diet.notes: Primarily an algae grazer and detritivore in the wild, but in captivity behaves as an opportunistic omnivore that will accept dried seaweed (nori), algae sheets, sinking pellets, frozen mysis, brine shrimp, and small pieces of meaty seafood. Supplement with calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace elements for healthy molts. If aquarium algae and detritus are insufficient, target-feed dried seaweed and the occasional meaty offering to prevent the crab from becoming opportunistic on tankmates.

goodWith: established reef and FOWLR tanks with strong flow and ample rockwork
fast, mid- to upper-water fish that stay clear of the bottom
larger, robust tankmates that the crab cannot opportunistically catch
avoidWith: small or slow-moving fish (gobies, blennies, firefish) once the crab grows large
ornamental shrimp and small inverts (sexy shrimp, anemone shrimp, small hermits)
small snails (potential prey at adult size)
other Sally Lightfoot Crabs in small tanks (territorial in tight quarters)

imageCandidates.notes: tato grasso, 2744x2058. Crisp top-down view of a single vibrant individual showing the flat disc-shaped carapace with reticulated markings, blue spots, and the diagnostic orange-to-yellow banded legs with red joints. Best diagnostic image.
tato grasso, 2788x2091. Sharp in-situ shot of two individuals on rock, clearly showing carapace pattern and orange/yellow leg banding; conveys the rocky-crevice habitat.

Diego Delso, 5205x3470. High-resolution, sharp frontal in-water view showing the flattened body wedged under a ledge, long banded legs with yellow tips and blue-purple claws; different angle for variety.

summary: A fast, flat-bodied crab native to the Caribbean and Atlantic, identifiable by its brown carapace and contrasting orange-to-yellow leg banding. Often kept in reef tanks as an algae and detritus grazer, though adults tend to become opportunistic toward smaller tankmates.
careNotes: Percnon gibbesi is a beginner-friendly saltwater scavenger commonly offered for cleanup-crew roles in reef and FOWLR tanks. The aquarium-trade animal is the Atlantic/Caribbean Sally Lightfoot — the famous Galapagos shore crab (Grapsus grapsus) sometimes called the 'true' Sally Lightfoot is a different species and is not in the aquarium trade. Sources broadly agree on the husbandry envelope: roughly 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, and SG 1.023–1.025 — well within standard reef conditions. Adults reach about 3 cm carapace width with a 7–10 cm leg-tip-to-leg-tip span; the flat carapace lets them wedge into small rock crevices, so provide abundant live rock with ledges and cracks for shelter. They prefer strong flow and an active, scurrying foraging style, picking algae and detritus continuously with their front claws. Reef compatibility is conditional and size-dependent — small juveniles are reliably reef-safe algae grazers, but adults frequently become opportunistic and have been documented grabbing small or slow fish (gobies, firefish, blennies), ornamental shrimp, and small snails. Flag this risk before housing the crab with delicate cleanup-crew or small fish. Like all marine crustaceans they are copper-sensitive and intolerant of copper-based medications, and require calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace element supplementation for clean molts. They will also climb agilely on rock and equipment, so a well-fitted lid is recommended.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Provided care level (easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), water parameters (72–78°F / 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025), diet (algae plus meaty supplements), maximum size (3 inches), reef-compatibility caveat (becomes aggressive at large size and can catch small invertebrates and fish), and supplement needs (Ca, Mg, I, trace elements).

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Sally Lightfoot Crab Species Profile
additional.notes: Secondary swap: the assigned secondary (Reef2Reef wiki root) returned HTTP 403 and there is no dedicated Reef2Reef wiki page for this species. AquariumDomain was substituted as the next available specialist secondary. Cross-checked all water parameters (identical to LiveAquaria), gave a 12 US gal minimum tank floor (~45 L), confirmed semi-aggressive reef-compatible status, family Grapsidae (older taxonomy), and Indo-Pacific/Atlantic/Caribbean origin.

additional.name: Reeflings — Sally Lightfoot Crab (Percnon gibbesi) Care Guide
additional.notes: Third-source cross-check used to resolve tank-size and lifespan spread. Adult size 8 cm / 3" leg-tip span, minimum tank 100 L / 26 US gal, temperature 24–27°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.023–1.025, semi-aggressive with potential to prey on small fish and shrimp, several-year lifespan. The 100 L floor (vs AquariumDomain's 12 gal) drove the larger tank-size figure used here, in line with the playbook's cautious-larger rule for active rockwork-foraging species.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Percnon gibbesi
additional.notes: Taxonomic source. Modern family is Percnidae (the species was historically placed in Plagusiidae and is still listed under Grapsidae by some hobby sources). Native range covers the Atlantic (Florida to Brazil; Madeira to the Gulf of Guinea), the Eastern Pacific (California to Chile), and the Mediterranean as an established invasive (first recorded at Linosa, Sicily in 1999). Carapace width to ~30 mm; strictly herbivorous in the wild — unusual for a temperate crab.

additional.name: Reef Builders — The Sally Lightfoot Crab: A Real Character of a Crustacean
additional.notes: Hobbyist editorial. Confirmed ~4-inch leg-tip-to-leg-tip span, subtidal habitat at roughly 0–4 m (used as the lower bound of the depth range here; ~15 m used as an upper bound to cover deeper rocky reef occurrences reported in invasive-range surveys). Confirmed strictly-herbivorous wild diet and flat carapace adapted to wedging into rocky crevices.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Like other small grapsoid-style crabs, Percnon gibbesi molts roughly every four to eight weeks as a juvenile and less often as a fully grown adult, with the frequency gradually declining as the crab matures. Stable calcium, magnesium, and trace element levels support shell hardening after each molt, and a cuttlebone or mineral block can help supply the minerals needed; sudden salinity or temperature swings should be avoided, as they can trigger failed molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not documented in the aquarium trade. Wild larvae are long-lived planktonic stages — a major contributor to the species' Mediterranean invasiveness — which makes home rearing impractical.
